figures.caption: Table 4 Imputation performance of MICE, BRITS and Bayes-MICE under two MCMC samplers (RWM/MALA). Metrics are NMAE and NRMSE (mean, standard deviation) across 30 experimental runs for four PhysioNet variables. Boldface highlights the best value per row.

Table 3 Imputation performance of MICE, BRITS and Bayes-MICE under two MCMC samplers (RWM/MALA). Metrics are NMAE and NRMSE (mean, standard deviation) across 30 experimental runs for six AirQuality variables. Boldface highlights the best value per row.

Figure 6: Posterior predictive performance of the proposed Bayes-MICE model over time for the environmental CO(GT) variable (top row) and the clinical Na variable (bottom row), shown across training(left) and test(right) sets. The blue lines represent observed values, the red lines indicate posterior predictive means, and the shaded regions denote the 95% credible intervals.
Figure 7: Comparative imputation accuracy and distribution across runs (box plot) for deterministic and Bayesian methods on the PhsyioNet dataset using NRMSE

Table 2 Convergence summaries for the bicarbonate (HCO3) variable from the PhysioNet dataset using Bayes-MICE. Results are shown for two MCMC samplers: MALA (left) and RWM (right). The table reports posterior means, standard deviations (SD), 94% highest density intervals (HDIs), effective sample sizes (ESS), and R̂ statistics.
Figure 3: Convergence diagnostics for τ2 (trace, marginal density, and ACF) across two chains and two samplers for one of the variables (HC03) from the physionet dataset.

Figure 5: Imputation error patterns over time index of each method across different datasets, with the AirQuality CO(GT) variable shown in the top panel and the PhysioNet HCO3 variable shown in the bottom panel

Figure 2: The proposed Bayes-MICE imputation framework. The framework consists of temporal pattern detection, placeholder initialisation, MICE loop with lagged predictors and Bayesian modelling, while MCMC (RWM or MALA) provides posterior sampling and parameter updates. The final imputation is generated via posterior predictive draws.

Figure 4: Prediction accuracy comparison for the CO(GT) variable from the AirQuality dataset (top) and the HCO3 variable from the physioNet dataset (bottom). Each subplot panel shows:(top-left) predicted versus true values, (top-right) residuals against the true values, (bottom-left) sorted true values with corresponding model predictions, and (bottom-right) box plots of absolute errors.

Table 1 Imputation performance comparison of univariate time-lagged MICE (lag = 1) with established time series baseline methods on a representative variable (WBC) from the PhysioNet dataset. We report RMSE, MAE, NMAE, and NRMSE, Boldface highlights the best value among the methods.
Figure 1: Time-lagged imputation mechanism in univariate MICE, where a missing value at time index t is imputed by conditioning on observed neighbouring values from past and future lags.

Table 5 Convergence summaries for the CO(GT) variable from the AirQuality dataset using Bayes-MICE. Results are shown for two MCMC samplers: MALA (left) and RWM (right). The table reports posterior means, standard deviations (SD), 94% highest density intervals (HDIs), effective sample sizes (ESS), and R̂ statistics.

Table 6 Ablation study comparing Bayes-MICE-V1 and Bayes-MICE-V2 on the AirQuality and PhysioNet datasets under the RWM sampler. Metrics are NMAE and NRMSE (mean, standard deviation) across 30 runs. Boldface indicates the better-performing variant for each variable within each dataset.
